text: Howdy, tank bottoms. Cowboy Crude to the rescue!

quotedTweet.text:
I used to be in charge of all the crude and product tank inventories in the refinery. 

Here is a simple picture of the operating levels of the tank. You've got deadstocks at the bottom of the tank because the pump nozzle is positioned slightly above a certain level. We used to call this 'the unpumpables'. Go below this level and your feed pump would cavitate. Big no-no.

Then you get a minimum working level after catering for contingency levels (e.g. enough product stocks to cater for x days of unplanned shutdown).

And although you have a huge tank that can technically fill to the brim, the actual working volume at the top is lower than the absolute max to also factor in for contingency levels (e.g. enough capacity to store off-spec rundown for an unplanned shutdown). Tank top scenario is a horror nobody wants to face due to risk of loss of primary containment (i.e. a safety incident).

In the current low stocks environment in the US, you are going to 'scrape tank bottoms', up to the minimum working level. So you will always have some stocks  because you can't draw to absolute zero.

The Story of Cowboy Crude

Howdy! I heard Mr Crude from the Middle East gave you folks a version of his story last month. Now it's my turn.

I'm called WTI, aka West Texas Intermediate. I'm light and sweet, unlike Mr Crude that is medium and sour. 

My close relative is Forties from the North Sea - that guy is also known as Brent simply because he typically sets the price of Dated Brent. His cousin, the REAL Brent Crude gets quite annoyed as this confusion!

So Brent and I have a love-hate relationship. We are physical oil and we are also paper benchmarks. That's hell of a lot confusing for many people. But what matters is I am super powerful in the market nowadays, to the point that I could even set the price of Dated Brent! (Take that, cousin Forties!)

Anyway the world is in trouble now. Mr Crude is still stuck, not much moving out from the Strait of Hormuz. I'm happily being exported out of the USGC at a rate of about 5 mbd, depending on who's the best bidder. Korea and Japan are doing a pretty good job at securing my lot to make up for whatever they are missing. 

But I'm hearing it's still not enough. Who cares? I'll just gallop my way and keep on winning. Based on Sparta's Arbs Radar, it looks like I'm still the cheapest barrel around in town.

Yours truly,
Cowboy Crude 

P/S: Just a friendly reminder I don't make much diesel and fuel oil like Mr Crude does.

The International Energy Agency (IEA) has described the war between the United States and Iran and severe limits on oil exports through the Strait of Hormuz as the largest supply disruption in the history of the oil market.  

But the impact has been far less severe so far than the oil shocks of the 1970s (or the later shocks of the late 2000s and early 2010s). Price increases have been smaller, briefer, and impacted a much narrower range of sectors. 

The short-term and long-term impacts on oil consumption are therefore likely to be much less than after the earlier oil shocks ...

Interesting US flow dynamics. 

PADD 2 is getting sucked dry as Gulf pulls barrels to export. Makes sense considering that PADD 2 has Canadian heavy sour barrels that are in demand right now. 

PADD 3 has SPR and enough stockpile to last through June.

But what’s really worth highlighting here is the PADD 2 and 4, landlocked, crude imports are falling. Land imports have dropped to 3.129 million b/d, normally over 3.4 million b/d. 

Cushing is already near tank bottom, so the Gulf can no longer pull barrels from inland to export, which means the incoming crude draws will be mostly in PADD 3. Canada doesn’t have enough barrels to export to the US now so flows will remain low. 

As PADD 3 crude inventories drop and as SPR flow rate slows, US crude exports will inevitably drop. This will force oversea markets to tighten. 

Right now, the market is making the binary assumption that once this MOU is signed, normality is the base case. Can the market overlook tank bottom and see that flows are increasing and prevent an oil price spike?

Sure, anything is possible. 

But to assume perfect execution and a smooth transition is wishful thinking considering the adversary here, Iran, knows the same math/logistical timeline as the rest of us. 

Balancing on a knife’s edge is probably the best way to describe this situation.

text: Precious metals steady as oil-driven inflation fears fade https://t.co/1PvEglFWNg via @saxobank https://t.co/asinEyQV2b

The gap between OPEC on one hand and the EIA and IEA on the other regarding the Middle East disruption's impact on 2026 oil demand has widened to around 2 million barrels per day. While OPEC continues to project demand growth close to pre-crisis trends, both the EIA and IEA now foresee outright demand contraction as elevated energy costs, weaker economic activity and supply disruptions weigh on consumption. The divergence, equivalent to roughly one Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) cargo per day, highlights an unusually wide split in how the world's leading energy forecasters assess the lasting economic fallout from the region's biggest supply shock in decades. 

Worth noting, however, is that all three agencies expect a strong rebound in demand next year. Forecast growth for 2027 currently ranges from 1.73 million barrels per day according to OPEC to as much as 2.5 million barrels per day according to the EIA, suggesting broad agreement that the current weakness represents deferred rather than permanently lost demand.

text: Goldman Sachs cuts Brent oil forecast to $80 for Q4 and $75 for 2027, citing faster Gulf export normalization on US-Iran deal optimism. #Oil #Iran

📌 Market Watch

Citi just slashed its Brent forecast by $20 a barrel. The Fed isn’t following yet — but its own logic says it should.

Citi cut its Q3 Brent forecast from $95 to $75, and Q4 from $80 to $70 — a steep reversal after the US-Iran breakthrough lowered the risk of a lasting Hormuz disruption.

Show more 👇

In March, the Fed kept one 2026 cut in its outlook even while oil was climbing — judging that appropriate. Oil has since reversed entirely. The same logic that kept a cut on the table during the spike should keep it there now that the spike unwound.

Goldman: cut possible by end of Q3. The chain is mechanical — lower oil → lower headline CPI → less reason to hold.

June 17 dot plot likely shows zero cuts for 2026. Citi’s number says that’s already stale.

Goldman Sachs Oil Forecast Revision – 16 June 2026

🔹 Forecast Changes:

Brent Q4 2026: cut to $80/bbl (from $90)

Brent 2027 avg: cut to $75/bbl (from $80)

🔹 Supply Normalization: Gulf exports expected to return to pre‑conflict levels by end‑July 2026 — one month earlier than prior assumption. Faster normalization trims ~$10/bbl off late‑2026 fair value and ~$5/bbl off 2027.

🔹 Deal Background: Preliminary US‑Iran agreement (MoU) includes reopening Strait of Hormuz, ceasefire extension, and ~60‑day window for nuclear/sanctions talks. Formal signing expected mid‑to‑late June. Oil shipments already partially recovering via rerouting/tanker use.

Goldman Sachs lowers oil forecast as recovery expected to accelerate after US-Iran peace deal

The brokerage now assumes Gulf oil exports will return to pre-conflict levels by the end of July, roughly one month earlier than previously projected.

Read More:
https://t.co/3Tt8MBTlTQ

Economic War Diary - June 17 🗓️

Key Updates 📝

1- Pending deal prompts markets to price in supply return 🤝
Markets are increasingly pricing in a gradual return of oil supplies following the anticipated agreement between the US and Iran, amid signs that oil and gas flows through the Strait of Hormuz could resume and restrictions on Iranian exports may be lifted.

2- Washington to allow Iranian oil exports immediately after deal signing 📝
The WSJ, citing sources, reported that the US will allow Iran to immediately resume oil and fuel exports under the war-ending agreement. Sanctions relief on Iranian oil sales, including banking, shipping and insurance services linked to the oil trade, is also expected to take effect upon signing.

3- Iranian tanker fleet begins moving 🚢
Iran's oil tanker fleet has begun repositioning ahead of the expected implementation of the agreement. At least one very large crude carrier has reportedly crossed the U.S. naval blockade line, while other tankers are heading toward Iranian oil terminals to load cargoes.

4- 118 tankers carrying 75 million barrels await reopening of shipping lane 🛢️
Estimates suggest that around 118 oil tankers carrying nearly 75 million barrels of crude remain stranded in the Gulf and are expected to begin moving once navigation through the Strait of Hormuz is fully restored.

5- Goldman Sachs cuts oil price forecasts 📉
Goldman Sachs lowered its oil price forecasts for late 2026 and 2027, projecting Brent crude at around $80 a barrel in the fourth quarter of this year, down from a previous forecast of $90, citing expectations of the Strait of Hormuz reopening and the return of supplies to the market.

6- Qatar aims to restore most gas exports within two months 🇶🇦
Bloomberg reported that Qatar plans to rapidly resume LNG production after the Strait of Hormuz reopens, targeting output of about 50% of operating capacity within one month and 80% within two months.

7- Renewed oversupply concerns weigh on oil market ⚖️
Dubai's prompt crude premium slipped into negative territory for the first time since early 2026 after reaching record levels in March. The move reflects easing supply concerns amid expectations of additional barrels returning to the market and oil flows resuming through the Strait of Hormuz.

8- HSBC: Full restoration of Hormuz flows may take until September ⏳
HSBC said oil traffic through the Strait of Hormuz is unlikely to normalize before late July and may not return to pre-war levels until the end of September, citing challenges related to mine clearance, insurance coverage and restarting production.

9- Chinese refiners cut output to four-year low 🇨🇳
Chinese refiners reduced crude processing last month to the lowest level in nearly four years after the country's crude imports fell to an eight-year low, highlighting the impact of the crisis on both demand and supply.

10- Continued drawdown of US oil reserves 🇺🇸
Data showed the US continued releasing crude from its strategic petroleum reserve, with around 8.89 million barrels sold into the market last week, supporting supply availability and easing pressure on domestic markets.

11- Gas flows to US export terminals rise 🏭
Natural gas deliveries to US LNG export plants increased 13.2% week-on-week to a six-week high of 19.3 billion cubic feet per day, despite ongoing seasonal maintenance activity weighing on monthly averages.

Key Indicators 📊

12- Oil ↓ 🛢️
Brent crude futures opened Wednesday trading below $80 a barrel, while US WTI crude fell below $77 a barrel, pressured by expectations of higher supplies following the anticipated US-Iran peace agreement.

13- Natural Gas ↓ 🔥
US natural gas futures, based on the Henry Hub benchmark, opened Wednesday trading at $3.25 per million British thermal units.

European natural gas futures, based on the Dutch TTF benchmark, opened at EUR 41.7 per megawatt-hour.

14- US gasoline prices ↓ ⛽
The average US gasoline price fell to $3.990 per gallon on Tuesday from $4.006 a day earlier, according to GasBuddy data. Before the war began, the average stood at $2.83 per gallon.

15- Shipping ↓ ⚓
The Baltic Dirty Tanker Index, which tracks crude tanker freight rates, extended losses on Tuesday, falling 0.62% to 1,927 points.

Takeaways  📌

Markets continue to shift from managing the fallout of the war to pricing in the return of supplies, as signs grow that a US-Iran agreement is nearing implementation and oil and gas flows through the Strait of Hormuz are set to resume. 

This has weighed on oil prices and strengthened expectations of a return to oversupply in the coming months, while Iranian tankers begin moving and major producers prepare to increase exports. However, estimates from shipping companies and banks suggest that a full restoration of normal trade flows could take several weeks or even months, meaning the effects of the crisis are likely to remain visible across energy and shipping markets despite fading geopolitical risks.

【美股】

隔夜美股三大指数分化：道指涨0.64%，标普500跌0.57%，纳指跌1.15%。

市场不是简单进入“风险关闭”，而是在成长股高估值、利率预期和盈利兑现之间重新定价。

科技成长方向波动加大，半导体、云计算和部分AI链条回调；价值股与防御属性资产阶段性托底。

AI资本开支仍是全球科技投资主线。

机构预计，未来数年AI与数据中心融资需求仍将活跃，2030年前相关总支出可能达到万亿美元级别。

数据中心液冷、私有云AI、高速互联、服务器、PCB、光模块和存储链条仍是重点观察方向。

日本经济数据偏强。

A股可关注自动化设备、机器人、出口链条的比较优势。

黄金逻辑进入“央行配置支撑”与“利率压力”并存阶段。

策略上更适合回调观察、冲高控仓，而不是追逐单日波动。

映射方向：山东黄金、紫金矿业、赤峰黄金、中金黄金。

原油是隔夜最大波动源。

WTI原油收跌5.82%至76.05美元/桶，布伦特原油收跌5.06%至78.96美元/桶，SC原油夜盘跌3.93%。

美联储决议今晚进入关键窗口。

若表述偏鹰，成长股估值、贵金属弹性可能继续受压；若强调继续观察数据，风险资产可能获得阶段性喘息。

美元指数报99.540，跌0.09%。

【国内线索】

2026陆家嘴论坛今日开幕，金融政策信号进入观察窗口。

市场关注资本市场改革、人民币国际化、金融支持科技创新、保险资金入市与风险防控等方向。

宽基ETF增量和融资余额回升，对券商、银行、保险、金融科技和高股息方向形成短线支撑。

苹果规划AI可穿戴新品，终端侧AI从手机延伸至耳机、眼镜和多传感器设备。

产业链关注光学、声学、结构件、低功耗芯片。

1. 原油急跌后的二次确认：IEA月报、EIA库存、成品油库存。

次日02:30：美联储召开新闻发布会

【一句话总结】

今天市场的核心不是单一风险偏好变化，而是“原油风险溢价回落 + 美联储决议定价 + AI资本开支继续扩张 + 国内金融政策窗口打开”的多线再平衡。

短线交易请控制杠杆与仓位，避免围绕单一消息追涨杀跌。

仅供研究学习，不构成投资建议。

By systematically targeting Russia’s primary economic engine—its energy and refining infrastructure—Kyiv has successfully pierced Moscow's home-front air defenses, forcing the Kremlin to choose between frontline military logistics and domestic economic stability. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
Current Assessment: Drone Campaign vs. Oil Infrastructure
Ukraine's unmanned aviation strategy has shifted from localized tactical strikes to a cumulative, long-range campaign targeting oil refineries, export hubs, and storage depots. [8, 9, 10]
Capacity Degradation: The drone blitz has damaged or temporarily decommissioned approximately 13.5% to 20% of Russia's total oil refining capacity. Major facilities, including the massive Ryazan refinery and Tatneft's flagship Taneco refinery in Tatarstan, have suffered major operational disruptions. [3, 11, 12, 13, 14]

Crude Processing Plunge: Russian crude oil processing fell to a 16-year low. Total crude output dropped by roughly 460,000 barrels per day compared to the prior year, severely choking oil product flows through critical Baltic and Black Sea ports. [11, 15, 16]
Domestic Fuel Shortages & Rationing: The Russian Energy Ministry publicly acknowledged that drone strikes are directly causing severe fuel supply issues. Major retail networks have introduced historic gasoline and diesel purchase limits (as low as 20 liters per vehicle). Widespread fuel shortages are reported in Crimea, southern Russia, and the Moscow suburbs. [14, 17, 18, 19, 20]
The State of Russia's War Economy

The economic cushion built by Moscow through high energy prices and sanction-evasion schemes is rapidly depleting under the weight of an escalating war budget. [21, 22]
GDP and Stagnation: After initial defense-fueled growth, the Russian economy officially contracted in the early months of this year. Senior Kremlin officials have conceded that economic growth has stalled, flatlining for the remainder of the year. [2, 4, 5]
Sovereign Funds Evaporating: The liquid assets of Russia’s National Wealth Fund have plummeted to just 1.8% of GDP, down from 6.5% at the onset of the invasion. [21, 22]

Spiraling Budget Deficits: The federal budget deficit exceeded the government's full-year target within the first three months alone. The total deficit is projected to surpass $28 to $29 billion. This shortfall is driven by a massive 45% collapse in first-quarter oil and gas revenues combined with a 30% surge in military spending. [5, 21, 22, 23, 24]
Corporate and Civilian Strain: Civilian manufacturing is declining at its fastest rate since 2022. Over 200,000 small and medium-sized enterprises closed in the first quarter due to sky-high central bank interest rates, currency volatility, labor shortages, and an aggressive increase in war-financing taxes. [2, 25, 26]

Predictive Analysis & Outlook
1. Logistics Lockdowns and Frontline Attrition

As Ukraine maintains a high volume of long-range launches, Russia's military logistics will face a severe bottleneck. Drone operations in intermediate zones are already inhibiting Russian troop transport and supply lines. With refineries in the Moscow and Volga regions frequently knocked offline, the Russian military will have to reroute fuel supplies through slower, vulnerable networks, limiting its offensive momentum. [1, 9, 15, 19, 20]
2. Looming Domestic Energy Emergency
To stabilize domestic supply and keep gas stations from running empty, Russia will likely be forced to enforce total bans on diesel and jet fuel exports. Moscow is projected to pivot from a premier global energy exporter to a seaborne fuel importer, sourcing refined petroleum via alternative paths or relying heavily on Belarus. This shift will irreversibly drain its remaining hard currency reserves. [1, 20, 27]
3. Hyper-Inflation and Severe Central Bank Interventions [9, 28]
To combat the budget deficit and the economic damage caused by the strikes, the Bank of Russia will keep interest rates prohibitively high to suppress domestic consumption and inflation. This ensures a prolonged recession in non-defense sectors, a sharp drop in real household incomes, and a cascade of bankruptcies among small and medium-sized civilian businesses. [2, 5, 26, 29, 30]
4. Rising Domestic Dissent and Political Strain
For the first time, cracks are forming in the Kremlin's domestic political consensus. Lawmakers in the State Duma have publicly warned that the combination of demographic strain, high war costs, and rampant drone strikes is pushing the country toward a "social explosion." While Vladimir Putin retains strict narrative control through state media, visible fuel lines and scaled-back public events indicate that the domestic economic costs of the war can no longer be hidden from the Russian public.
